# Japan Trip — April 2026

Two weeks, three bases (Tokyo, Kyoto, Kanazawa) plus a mountain detour.
Cherry-blossom timing is a gamble; everything below works with or without
the bloom.

## Logistics

Flights land at Haneda on April 2, depart Haneda April 16. The national
rail pass no longer pencils out for this route — buying individual
shinkansen legs is about ¥8,000 cheaper than the 14-day pass at current
prices. Reserve the Tokyo→Kyoto and Kanazawa→Tokyo legs in advance; the
Kyoto→Kanazawa Thunderbird is turn-up-and-go. Get Suica cards into Apple
Wallet before leaving — they work for nearly all city transit and most
konbini purchases.

## Tokyo (April 2–7)

Base in Kuramae — walkable to Asakusa without the crowds-at-dawn problem.
Planned anchors: Tsukiji outer market breakfast, teamLab on a weekday
morning, a Yanaka cemetery-and-temples walk, day trip to Kamakura for the
Daibutsu and the Hase-dera hydrangea path (too early for hydrangeas, still
worth it). One unplanned day held in reserve — Tokyo punishes
over-scheduling.

## Kyoto (April 7–11)

Base near Gion. Fushimi Inari at 6:30am or not at all; by 9 the lower gates
are a queue. Arashiyama early on a weekday, then north to Daitoku-ji's
quieter sub-temples in the afternoon. Booked a tea ceremony in a Nishijin
machiya for the 9th, and Kaiseki dinner on the last night.

## Koyasan detour (April 11–12)

The night that anchors the whole trip: a temple stay (shukubo) at
Eko-in on Mount Koya. Cable car up from Gokurakubashi, arrive before 16:00
for the temple's evening services. Dinner is shojin ryori — the monastery's
multi-course Buddhist vegetarian cuisine — served in-room. The 6:00am
morning prayer service and the fire ritual are open to guests; the night
walk through Okunoin cemetery among the lanterns is the single thing I am
most looking forward to. Book exactly one night; two is widely reported as
one too many.

## Kanazawa (April 12–15)

Thunderbird from Kyoto. Kenroku-en garden at opening time, the samurai and
geisha districts, Omicho market for lunch, and the 21st Century Museum.
Kanazawa is the rain-friendly base — most anchors are indoors-adjacent.

## Food rules

No reservations except the two Kyoto dinners. Lunch is wherever the line
of office workers is. One konbini-breakfast minimum per city — this is
non-negotiable.
